data.title: Summit Therapeutics: Not Pharmacyclics, Not Yet
data.description:
Summit Therapeutics Topline Summary and Update

Summit Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SMMT) has presented us with one of the biggest hype stories of the biologic's era in cancer medicine, surging out of nowhere to challenge the king behemoths in immunotherapy. "Summit Beats Merck" has been the singular impossible-to-ignore headline over the past year, kicking off an entire conversation about the role of bispecific antibodies, Chinese agents, and the future of pembrolizumab.

And there are a few skeptical voices about the prospects of ivonescimab and the investment thesis for SMMT as it stands in 2025. I am on record as being one of them, and that bearish sentiment, if you want to call it that, has not proven right by any stretch. Today, we got some news that CEO Bob Duggan and his co-CEO Maky Zanganeh exercised their warrants early to acquire nearly 4 million more shares each of the company's stock, which was seen as a strong bullish signal by the markets.

Today, I want to offer some updated thoughts on the company at this stage, highlighting why I think the risk at these levels outweighs the potential reward, despite the many catalysts and the rampant speculation that have driven the meteoric rise of this company.

Big Questions for SMMT – Is HARMONi-2 Really Relevant?

The thesis for SMMT is driven entirely by its stake in the bispecific antibody ivonescimab, which simultaneously binds and blocks PD-1 (the same target as blockbuster antibodies like pembrolizumab and nivolumab) and VEGF (the target of Roche's venerable antibody bevacizumab).

The main thrust of this company's current success lies mainly in the findings from a single study, HARMONi-2, which has been covered extensively by different analysts on Seeking Alpha, including me back when they were first reported. In short, this was the first study to show that a novel drug given alone could beat pembrolizumab in the first-line setting of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), with a near doubling of the progression-free survival.

More recently, we saw the formal publication of these results in The Lancet, offering a glimpse at more data. My hope is that a careful read of these findings will help to answer the following questions:

  1. Did patients live longer and better with ivonescimab?
  2. Are the results translatable to patients outside of China?

For question 1, we have some good and some bad news. First and foremost, overall survival data are not mature, and the authors do not present even an immature readout of those findings, so it remains to be seen whether ivonescimab improves arguably the most important outcome that will determine uptake of the drug in a crowded lung cancer space.

That said, I would be very surprised to see overall survival fail when we have this magnitude of benefit in terms of PFS.

Something else to consider, however, is that the standard of care for NSCLC is not pembrolizumab in patients who do not have high PD-L1 (50% or higher by TPS), and that was over half of the patients in HARMONi-2. So exactly 58% of the patients in both arms would be patients who do not qualify for pembrolizumab alone.

Of course, they do break these findings down by PD-L1 subgroup, and median PFS for patients with PD-L1 50% or higher appeared to benefit from ivonescimab treatment, as well (median PFS, 11.1 vs. 8.2 months). And the unstratified hazard ratio is still 0.48, so this is similar to the benefit seen in the full population. But this underperforms what you would expect from pembrolizumab alone in these patients, which is a median of more like 10.3 months from the KEYNOTE-042 trial.

Why does this matter if we're not allowed to make cross-trial comparisons? The PD-L1 “high” subgroup of patients being less than half of the study population means that most of the patients were not receiving the standard of care we would accept in the United States. And small numbers are more frequently associated with “unusual” results, including underperformance of a control arm.

Consider, for example, that SMMT is running the HARMONi-7 trial designed to confirm the benefit seen in HARMONi-2, and this trial is enrolling only patients with high PD-L1 expression. If this study powered to answer the relevant question of pembrolizumab vs. ivonescimab shows similar results for ivonescimab, but the control arm performs as expected from historical data, then the likelihood of a significant difference goes down.

Other notable issues with respect to HARMONi-2 include the following:

  1. More patients in the pembrolizumab arm had brain metastases (17% vs. 20%); HARMONi-7 will not allow symptomatic or large CNS metastases
  2. More patients in the pembrolizumab arm were older than 65 (51% vs. 58%)
  3. Slightly more patients in the ivonescimab arm had very high PD-L1 expression (11% vs. 9%)

These are small differences, of course, but small differences can make or break trials. And some readers have commented that I've droned on so much about the questions that remain from HARMONi-2 when SMMT has never said they would pursue approval based only on those data. I fully agree.

However, these discrepancies and questions are the very reason that I will not be surprised if a properly designed study fails to recapitulate the Chinese study. It is an important risk that you cannot discount yet.

And note that there are quite a few Chinese clinical trials that failed to end up being reproduced in a manner acceptable for ex-China use. This is why we do not prescribe afatinib for gastric cancer or donafenib for liver cancer.

The Numerous Other Threads Being Developed for Ivonescimab

SMMT and Akeso are not setting their sights only on pembrolizumab's spot in NSCLC. This would be an important one to knock off the pedestal, of course, but the companies are being very aggressive in initiating pivotal trials across a variety of indications, all representing other shots on goal for the drug.

Here's a brief list of the various "major" clinical trials that have been activated:

  • HARMONi-3 and -6: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy vs. pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for squamous cell NSCLC
  • HARMONi-GI1: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy for biliary tract cancer
  • SOLO-10: Ivonescimab vs. pembrolizumab for PD-L1-positive head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
  • HARMONi-BC1: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy for triple-negative breast cancer

And others are planned or activated in settings like pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

All of this provides a semblance of balance to the questions I'm raising about the strength of the results we've seen gain a lot of hype. The companies have put many, many irons in the fire, and this positions them to find a niche for ivonescimab if the HARMONi-7 trial does not confirm the initial results. Or, if HARMONi-7 is successful, these trials give the companies a platform for very rapid expansion into other tumor areas.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2024, SMMT held $104.9 million in cash and equivalents, as well as another $307.5 million in short-term investments. Operating expenses reached $226.3 million, with R&D expenses surging from $59.5 million to $150.8 million between 2023 and 2024, underscoring the aggressive expansion of research efforts.

After interest, the net loss for the year was $221.3 million. The actual cash used for operations was $142.1 million for 2024, implying a cash runway of between 2 and 3 years. Notably, SMMT raised over $430 million via private placements for the year.

Bottom-Line Summary: Why I Remain Both Skeptical and Hopeful

The impetus for today's article was the aforementioned early exercise of warrants by the company's chief executives, which was viewed as a roaring bullish signal by the markets. Why would the man who holds 70% of SMMT's outstanding shares buy more at these levels? Because he believes they're going to go up and the market wants in on that. Seeking Alpha reported that this is likely in anticipation of an overall survival readout for HARMONi-2, which many are expecting to be positive.

There's no arguing that confidence from the CEO of a company is being demonstrated when they acquire more shares of the company. And for some, these kinds of signals are all the news you need. The main person in question here is a legendary investor, a genius behind the success of Pharmacyclics and their multi-billion-dollar success. What he touches is gold.

But when it comes to whether SMMT is a good investment at these levels, I think it is worth considering that the market is pricing in success like SMMT itself prints money already. At a market cap of around $17 billion, if SMMT were allowed to have Merck's price-to-sales ratio, they are already being priced in at having $5 billion in sales. Pembrolizumab, which has over 40 approved indications, generates sales of almost $30 billion per year. And you can mark my words that the journey for ivonescimab will be messier than a complete overthrow of pembrolizumab overnight.

In essence, the market is telling me right now that SMMT has in its hands 1/6 of the market that pembrolizumab has. It just does not resonate with reality. And while I admit that reality frequently disagrees with what I, a singular, anonymous contributor on Seeking Alpha, view as reasonable, it is important to understand why this project is far from the guarantee that the market is pricing in.

Make no mistake: ivonescimab is one of the most important emerging stories to come out of 2024 and 2025, and it could very well be a disruptive force in oncology. But to expect that it means SMMT should command buyout prices on the level of Celgene, Genentech or Shire before the company has ever sold the drug rings as highly premature.

If you are a fan of the developing story that is SMMT, I think that's justified. I would personally not endorse leveraging funds into the story at this time, not without further proof of principle. The downside risk is too high that a single marginal or negative data readout could burst the entire bubble, and the findings from HARMONi-2 on their own do not go far enough to justify this yet, not while there are important questions unanswered. Therefore, selling some of a position that one has been holding would be a justified move as well.

data.title: TSMC Earnings Preview, AI Strength Faces Tariff Headwinds
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (NYSE: TSM) reports Q1 2025 Earnings on April 17, 2025 in the post-market. Today, we already received Q1 revenue figures that were slightly ahead of analyst estimates at $25.6 billion. AI server chips and smartphone chips were key items. I think it is currently attractive to buy a short-term strangle on TSM, and the main reasons are: volatility due to tariffs could remain elevated, earnings will keep the value of the strangle elevated, and the market anticipates a relatively big move on earnings; however, I think that is likely still an underestimate.

Especially TSM, its March sales were strong and this is likely a result of the tariffs that were announced on April 2nd. Canlys reported only yesterday exactly this happening with PC sales. This likely means channels have been stuffed, even though the tariffs were largely put on hold yesterday as well. Some of the future demand has now been front-loaded. In addition, the tariffs are taken very seriously, as evident from the market action in the past few days. It is very hard to see how these, even in a pared down version, won't have an effect on global trade.

The AI trend is critical to TSM. CEO Wei has said that revenue from AI products made up a mid-teens percent of total revenue in 2024. A 3x increase from the year before. He's also indicated it should double in 2025. The firm sees a mid-40% compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) over the next five years for AI and 20% overall revenue growth (emphasis by me):

Underpinned by our technology leadership and broad customer base, we now forecast the revenue growth from AI accelerators to approach a mid-40% CAGR for the five-year period, starting off the already higher base of 2024. We expect AI accelerators to be the strongest driver of our HPC platform growth and the largest contributor in terms of our overall incremental revenue growth in the next several years.

Looking ahead, as the world's most reliable and effective capacity provider, TSMC is playing a critical and integral role in the global semiconductor industry. With our technology leadership, manufacturing excellence and customer trust, we are well positioned to address the growth from the industry megatrend of 5G, AI and HPC with our differentiated technologies. For the five-year period starting from 2024, we expect our long-term revenue growth to approach a 20% CAGR in the U.S. dollar term, fueled by all four of our growth platforms, which are smartphones, HPC, IoT and automotive.

Especially, if the 40% AI or 20% overall growth rate over the next 5 years comes down, that could hurt the stock.

So far, there isn't a lot of evidence that the AI-Capex boom is slowing down. Admittedly, Microsoft already said (MSFT) it would slow its AI-Capex spending before the tariff announcement. But Alphabet (GOOG) confirmed it would go ahead with $75 billion in spending for 2025 today. Meta Platforms (META) was still working on a $1 billion datacenter deal as recently as April 4th.

TSM could be guarded in their guidance. It might not want to address the tariff chaos impact on its long-term outlook. If anything, just to avoid getting called out by the President. On the other hand, the firm could use the opportunity to communicate about the threat of tariffs, global trade war and dissuade the administration from being overly zealous.

A clear signpost of expectations will be TSM's own CapEx. TSM previously guided spending between $38 billion and $42 billion. If that guidance is adjusted downwards, it means the firm is expecting less robust demand (emphasis mine):

Next, let me talk about our 2025 capital budget and depreciation. Every year our CapEx is spent in anticipation of the growth that will follow in the future years, and our CapEx and capacity planning are based on the long-term market demand profile. At TSMC, a higher level of capital expenditures is always correlated with higher growth opportunities in the following years. In 2024, we spent $29.8 billion, as we continue to invest to support our customers' growth. With our strong technology leadership and differentiation, we are well-positioned to capture the multi-year structure demand from the industry megatrends of 5G, AI and HPC.

In 2025, we expect our capital budget to be between $38 billion and $42 billion, as we invest to capture the future growth. Out of the $38 billion to $42 billion CapEx for 2025, about 70% of the capital budget will be allocated for advanced process technologies, about 10% to 20% will be spent for specialty technologies, and about 10% to 20% will be spent for advanced packaging, testing, mask making and others.

The options market is pricing in a large possible move for TSM around earnings. At least relative to its historical moves.

On one hand, that's a bit strange because they've already communicated revenue which came in slightly ahead of analyst expectations. It seems likely enough quarterly EPS will come in just ahead of expectations as well. Given its history of beats, the revenue figure and front-loaded inventory, I think it is not unreasonable to go with $2.1 per share.

I don't think it is the earnings number and beat that will matter, though. It is the guidance for the rest of the year (and beyond) on expected growth as well as the CapEx guidance that's going to matter.

Margins are likely to be solid to strong, and I don't think they will be a key point unless there's very negative guidance on them going forward.

When earnings events are coming up, I tend to look at the options to see what these seem to be communicating and whether there is something interesting to do. In this instance, the TSM strangle looks attractive to me.

In this case, the strangle looks sort of cheap to me outside of that expected move. Trump paused tariffs, but there is still a lot of potential for news around negotiations and how hard political entities will test the market what kind of tariffs or deals are acceptable.

Volatility in this strangle will remain at least somewhat elevated as long as we are ahead of the earnings event. It will rapidly decrease after the event, so if you own options through the event, carefully consider whether you want to hold on afterwards.

The strangle requires an 11.75% up or down move before it breaks even from a $158.72 share price. The call is a bit cheaper. This makes some sense to me. Historically, TSM has had no 1-day down moves over 5% going back to Q1 2022. With the recent volatile market action and tariff uncertainty, I'd expect there is a lot of room for a relief rally or a sharp drawdown in case of gloomy guidance. Add to that the potential for high volatility outside of the earnings event, and the straddle appears undervalued.

Keep in mind that a straddle will lose all of its value if the price doesn't exceed the break-even price at expiration. For investors not interested in options the takeaway is that this earnings event could be a lot more bumpy than it usually is. If that isn't desirable, or you have a strong directional view for the event, it could justify a resizing of the position.

data.title: Alphabet: Why AI-Powered YouTube Ad Revenue Is Its Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I give Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (TSX:GOOG:CA) a strong buy rating due to the company's excellent annual free cash flow, their high-quality earnings, their shares trading at a discount of $121.47, 45% below their intrinsic value, and their AI-powered YouTube ad revenue segment set to be a major catalyst for the company going forward and already experiencing an average 21.62% year-over-year [YoY] growth over the last seven years.

Background

I'm someone who watches a lot of YouTube. As a matter of fact, when it comes down to watching videos on streaming services, I would say that roughly 95% of my time goes to YouTube; the other 5% probably goes to Amazon Prime. So it makes sense that when I read some of the most recent news and data surrounding Alphabet on Seeking Alpha, I'll come across articles where investment firms, such as MoffetNathanson, have crowned YouTube as the 'New King of All Media.' And in another article, Nielsen noted last month that, even though TV watch time fell in February, streaming remains robust. And which streaming platform led the pack for all streaming services? You guessed it: YouTube.

Yet, what is interesting to note about most Seeking Alpha analysis done on Alphabet is how often the YouTube segment of revenue tends to go under the radar. Don't misunderstand me here: Alphabet is a huge company and has a lot going on. And there have been some very good articles written in prior months on their YouTube segment of revenue, such as The Techie's article "YouTube Could Be Google's Next Big Thing," and The Investing Fool's article "YouTube is A Lot Bigger Than You Think," where The Investing Fool also noted that YouTube's revenue for Alphabet often goes underreported.

But one growth catalyst that has not been discussed in depth for Alphabet is the cross between artificial intelligence [AI] and their YouTube ad segment of revenue, which has been a leading segment of YoY revenue growth for the company since 2018.

However, before I dive into my thesis, I want to start with a valuation, specifically using a discounted cash flow [DCF] model. An undervaluation will only make a growth catalyst article more juicy, given that the stock will have plenty of room to grow into its intrinsic value and be more convincing for the reader. So, let's dive into the numbers:

The Annual Free Cash Flow and Earnings Quality

Let's start with the company's annual free cash flow, taking the numbers from Alphabet's latest 10-K:

  • Cash provided by operating activities was $125.30 billion, a difference of almost 19% from last year's $101.75 billion.
  • Capital expenditures, primarily used for technical infrastructure to support the growth of Alphabet, such as investments in servers and network equipment for computing, storage, and networking requirements, as well as office facilities, development projects, and building improvements, totaled $52.50 billion, a jump of almost 39% from last year's $32.3 billion spent on capital expenditures.

The difference between these two numbers results in an annual free cash flow of $72.80 billion.

The company's net income sits at $100.12 billion. And with $25.18 billion more coming from cash provided by operating activities than in net income, the company has stellar high-quality earnings.

A Discounted Cash Flow

For my DCF model, I will be using the annual free cash flow as the primary metric using the following numbers and assumptions:

  • An annual free cash flow of $72.80 billion.
  • A long-term growth rate for five years of 18%. (This number comes from LSEG Data & Analytics Stocks Plus Report, expecting a five-year growth rate of 19.9%, and Seeking Alpha's three-to-five year long-term growth rate of 16.16%, with both numbers averaging out to 18%.)

Taking the company's annual free cash flow and multiplying it with its expected growth rate over the next five years gives us the numbers below:

  • Year 1 (2026): $85.90 billion
  • Year 2 (2027): $105.36 billion
  • Year 3 (2028): $124.33 billion
  • Year 4 (2029): $146.71 billion
  • Year 5 (2030): $173.12 billion

Next, I'll get the discount rate using CAPM with the following numbers:

  • The risk-free rate of 4.16% is taken from the yield on U.S. securities maturing in ten years.
  • We'll use an expected market return of 8.18% (this is taking the average market return and the real average market return [i.e., the market return adjusted for inflation] and averaging them both out).
  • A beta of 1.03% calculated from the company's beta over a five-year period:
    • 0.0416 + (0.0818 - 0.0416) x 1.03 = 0.083
    • CAPM Discount Rate of 8.3%

To get the company's residual value, I will take the cash flow from year five in 2030 above of $173.12 billion, set its terminal growth rate to 4%, and plug the numbers into the residual formula below:

  • (173.12 x (1 + 0.04)) ÷ (0.083 - 0.04)
  • $4.19 trillion = Residual Value

Finally, we'll calculate the present value of each and every future cash flow, with the results shown below:

  • Year 1 (2026): $79.32 billion
  • Year 2 (2027): $89.83 billion
  • Year 3 (2028): $97.88 billion
  • Year 4 (2029): $106.65 billion
  • Year 5 (2030): $116.20 billion
  • Residual: $2.81 trillion

Adding up all the years, we arrive at an intrinsic value of $3.30 trillion. For the last step, we'll get the number of outstanding shares (as of January 28, 2025) that consist of the following classes of stock:

  • 5.83 billion of Class A Stock
  • 860 million of Class B Stock
  • 5.50 billion of Class C Stock
    • 12.19 billion of total outstanding stock

Finally, we divide Alphabet's intrinsic value of $3.30 trillion by their 12.19 billion outstanding shares and arrive at an intrinsic value of $270.71, meaning the stock is currently undervalued by $121.47, almost 45% below its intrinsic value.

Comparing Discounts

I find it quite ironic that in the last article I wrote on Sprouts Farmers Market (NASDAQ:SFM), I noted that Sprouts was the most discounted stock I had evaluated on Seeking Alpha (mind you, I have only analyzed nine companies) since Sprouts was trading at a 42% discount below its intrinsic value. But now, with the S&P 500 on the verge of entering a bear market, and Alphabet down 23.11% YTD (as of the time of the writing of this article), Alphabet is now the most discounted stock I have evaluated on Seeking Alpha using my DCF model, with the stock trading at a 45% discount.

The reason I bring this up is because I noted in that same Sprouts article that, even though the S&P 500 has been beat up, all those stocks I've evaluated with massive discounts were up by double-digits. Sprouts, for example, is already up almost 5% since the time that article was published, while Coca-Cola, the second most discounted stock I've evaluated, is up 11.25% even though the S&P 500 is down -16.72% since that article was published.

In other words, stocks on discounts can be, though not necessarily, major buying opportunities, and I believe Alphabet is no exception. So now that we know Alphabet is very much undervalued, I want to explain how and why YouTube ads are the major growth catalyst that can drive Alphabet's shares higher and, eventually, into their intrinsic value as calculated above.

A Close Look at YouTube Ad Revenue

Let's break down Alphabet's revenue streams in 2024 to see what percentage is coming from YouTube:

As we see above, 10.3% of Alphabet's revenue comes from YouTube ads, and by dividing the $36.15 billion of YouTube ad revenue by the $264.59 billion total for all Google advertising, we see that 13.7% of Google's advertising revenue comes from YouTube ads. While 10.3% and 13.7% may not seem, at face value at least, impressive, note that it is only a few percentage points behind second place Google cloud and third place Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices.

But what is even more important, if we are talking about a growth catalyst, is analyzing the revenue percent change for YouTube ads over the last several years, since that will help us to understand how much YouTube ads are truly driving growth for the company. We'll start from 2017, since any official data before that listed Alphabet's YouTube ad revenue under "Google properties revenues" and lumped it together with other revenue streams.

The data in the table above doesn't lie: First, revenue for YouTube ads has increased by an impressive 21.62% average YoY for the last seven years. The most notable years were from 2020 to 2021, with YouTube ad revenue increasing by a whopping 45.93%, no doubt due in large part to the pandemic bringing in the most growth to YouTube in comparison to any other social media app.

And, second, though there is a tapering off of revenue growth from 2022 and 2023 into the single digits for the first time, Alphabet's latest 10-K shows that YouTube ad revenue growth has grown once again by double digits-14.72% to be exact. Now let's compare this to the revenue percent change for the Google search and other revenue segment that leads all other segments in terms of total revenue.

Once again, the data above doesn't lie: revenue for Google Search & other has increased by an average of 14.58% YoY, seven percent less than the 21.62% average for YouTube ad revenue. All this data means that YouTube ads are, statistically, where the momentum lies and, thus, where the catalyst lies.

How AI Will Drive Business from Google Search to YouTube

One reason why YouTube advertising is the catalyst for Alphabet's revenue going forward is simple: AI. Have you ever noticed that when your Google searches for a particular term or question, Google will first generate an AI response and give an AI overview detailing the answer, along with sources for its response, before any other search results appear? I often find myself astonished at the speed and accuracy of nearly all of the responses I receive.

An article in Winning WordPress predicts that, since AI is removing the need for manual searches given these instant, direct answers given by Google, Google search ads will decline while YouTube will absorb the attention and ad spend; although much remains to be seen, so far, I am in agreement with that prediction, especially with other competitors, such as ChatGPT, taking market share of global search queries as noted by Nexus Research in their latest article for Alphabet.

As a matter of fact, the data I've tabled above speaks for itself: In the last seven years, the only year where the Google Search & other revenue segment had a bigger YoY increase over YouTube ads was in 2022. Otherwise, YouTube ads have been the clear winner in terms of YoY revenue growth.

But will the YouTube ad segment of revenue continue its double-digit growth back into its average range of 21.62% YoY and, possibly once again, into the thirty to forty percentile that we last saw in 2021? My answer is yes-once again via AI.

AI-Powered Growth for YouTube Ad Revenue

Per Alphabet's latest Q4 earnings transcript, Senior Vice President [SVB] and Chief Business Officer [CBO] Philipp Schindler stated the following:

Based on the Nielsen meta analysis of marketing mixed models, on average, Google AI-powered video campaigns on YouTube deliver 17% higher return on advertising spend than manual campaigns. Turning to YouTube, we saw robust revenue growth backed by continued growth in watchtime across ad-supported and Premium experiences. Our focus here remains on building a streaming platform that enables creators to thrive and unlock the full potential of AI.

The Nielsen meta analysis Schindler is referring to in this transcript is this Nielsen case study that highlights Google's return on investment [ROI] of AI-powered advertising. In sum:

Before AI-powered advertisements, advertisers manually chose where to place their ads and where to target their demographics. But now, with AI streamlining the process-and with how new AI-powered solutions are to YouTube's advertising-Google needed a study to measure AI's effectiveness on the return on ad spend [ROAS] and sales effectiveness in comparison to manual placements. The campaign measured over 50,000 brand campaigns and over one million performance campaigns in the U.S., using Nielsoen Marketing Mix Modeling to gather the data.

The result was, as stated by Schindler above, that "Google AI-powered video campaigns on YouTube deliver 17% higher return on advertising spend than manual campaigns." AI-powered advertising also outperformed manual campaigns in sales effectiveness. Nielsen then concluded their study with the following statement:

AI isn't just a fad or a futuristic planning tool—it's a performance driver... it's undeniable that AI-powered campaigns can drive a bottom-line impact.

Thesis Risk

There are a number of risks to my thesis. For starters, AI-powered advertisements are not exclusive to Alphabet; other streaming services, such as Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX), and social media platforms, such as Meta (NASDAQ:META) and TikTok, are already making use of AI-powered advertising. These platforms are all fighting for our attention and, ultimately, our dollars. And if advertisers decide that one of Alphabet's competitors has better targeting, return on ad spend, or sales effectiveness, then that could hamper Alphabet's growth in this sector.

Another risk is listed on Alphabet's latest 10-K: regulation around data privacy. Alphabet, like any other company, is subject to government regulation. Taking the line directly from their latest 10-K:

Particularly with regard to AI, competition, consumer protection, content moderation, data privacy and security, news publications, and sustainability and other social matters, we have seen an increase in new and evolving laws and regulations, as well as related enforcement actions and investigations, being proposed and implemented in recent years by legislative and regulatory bodies around the world.

Thus, if there are governmental limitations imposed on how Alphabet collects their data, and their AI-powered advertising becomes less effective as a result, then this could also lead to advertisers giving business to one of Alphabet's competitors and hampering the company's growth.

And, of course, there is always the risk of an upcoming recession or economic slowdown that would no doubt have advertisers cut their marketing budgets and hamper this segment of revenue. I'll be honest here, this is the one risk that I believe most investors, such as myself, may be worried about given this year's volatile nature of the stock market and economic uncertainty revolving around President Trump's tariffs.

I wish I had a crystal ball and could predict the market to see whether or not this was the case. But, as of now, Alphabet's YouTube's AI-powered ad revenue seems set for growth and seems to be a major catalyst for the company going forward.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Federal Realty Investment Is Now In Buy Territory, Yielding Close To 5%
data.description:
The markets have been extremely volatile after Liberation Day. First, the administration rolled out tariffs, which caused the market to drastically decline. Next, the administration decided to push back tariffs for 90 days on a country-by-country basis, which fueled one of the largest rallies we have seen, only to be met with unpredictable volatility. While the S&P 500 gained 10.15% last week, it's still down by 4.32% over the past month and by 8.81% over the year. While just about every sector has been impacted, I have been looking for opportunities in sectors of the economy that have limited tariff exposure that can generate a larger dividend yield than the risk-free rate of return. Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) has been on my watch-list for several years, and after the recent drawdown, I am finally upgrading my investment thesis to bullish. FRT owns high-quality real estate assets that have allowed them to increase their dividends for more than 5 decades. The United States 10-year Bond Yield (US10Y) has increased to 4.49%, but the latest CPI report coming in at 2.4% while PPI declined by -0.4% in March makes me believe that the Fed is going to be forced to pivot and be less restrictive with monetary policy. When this occurs, it should provide a large catalyst for REITs in general while making their yields more attractive as the risk-free rate of return declines. I think that FRT is finally trading at a level that makes sense, and I will be watching it much closer as it is now a candidate that can generate both capital appreciation and large amounts of dividend income in the future.

Following up on my previous article about Federal Realty Investment

Just over a year ago, I wrote an article about FRT (can be read here), and my investment case was neutral. Despite the recent weeks, the S&P 500 has increased by 29.92% since that article was published, and FRT has declined by -9.01%. When the dividends are taken into consideration, FRT's total return over this period was -0.89%. I had discussed why I wanted to become a shareholder, but I felt the valuation was too expensive. FRT is a Dividend King, which is an accomplishment not many companies will ever achieve. Regardless of what the macroeconomic headwinds were, FRT has increased its dividend on an annual basis for the past 5-decades. I am following up with a new article as I am finally turning bullish on FRT. I think the valuation is now appealing, and we could see a big rally in REITs in the coming months. I also believe that the Fed is backed into a corner and will pivot before the Jackson Hole event over the summer. FRT could become a strong alternative for risk-free income as the Fed starts cutting rates again.

Risks to investing in Federal Realty Investment

While I am bullish on FRT, there are several risk factors to consider. Investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward FRT, as it may not be a great fit for their investment goals. The first risk is opportunity cost, as FRT has drastically underperformed the market, and the amount of dividend income it generates doesn't close the gap. Investors should make sure that they are investing in FRT for the right reasons, as this is primarily an income investment, and investors looking for a pure play on capital appreciation will likely be disappointed. As a REIT, FRT has more risks toward fluctuations in interest rates than other companies. If the Fed takes rates higher, it will increase the cost of capital, which could compress margins and potentially lower property values. As the global economy changes and e-commerce becomes a larger piece of the commerce landscape, some companies may look to reduce their physical footprint, which could leave locations vacant and cause FRT's occupancy rates to fall. If this occurs, FRT's revenue and funds from operations (FFO) are likely to decline. There are a lot of things to consider, and investors should do a lot of research.

I believe that the recent data is pushing the Fed toward cutting rates, which is bullish for FRT

Oil is trading for around $60 per barrel, and we just tied the lowest CPI print over the past 3-years at 2.4%. On a YoY basis, CPI has fallen from 3.5% to 2.4%, and PPI was negative for the first time in more than a year. Inflation is clearly getting closer to the Fed's neutral rate of 2%, and the impacts of oil trading around $60 haven't had time to work their way throughout the economy. While the Fed has continued to be hawkish, conventional wisdom regarding the historical impacts of rates is no longer as impactful as it once was. When I look at the correlation between the Fed Effective Rate and the 30-Year Fixed Mortgage going back to the early 1970s we have never been in a position like this. The Fed's Effective Rate has never been as low as it has been coming out of the great financial crisis or the pandemic since a brief period in 1960. The 30-year mortgage also hadn't been under 5% until rates dropped in 2010, and homeowners had more than a decade of the 30-year remaining under 5%. After the pandemic, the 30-year fell under 3% for a period of time before the Fed took rates past 5% and the costs of capital skyrocketed.

Now, we are in a situation where companies and individuals aren't looking forward to refinancing debt or taking out loans. Companies that borrowed money during the years of low rates could be forced to refinance at significantly higher rates, which would compress margins as the cost of servicing the debt increases. This could cause unemployment to increase and a recession to occur. The Fed has a dual mandate where its obligations are split between price stability and maximum employment. Prices are declining, and inflation is close to its natural rate, yet the Fed has kept the effective rate at restrictive levels compared to where it has been. We can no longer look at historical rates going back to the 1980s because companies have been borrowing capital when the Fed had an effective rate under 1%, which had never happened before 2010. I think that the Fed is watching the numbers closely and is now backed into a corner because they can't use the excuse of inflation much longer, and they certainly don't want to be late to the party and be the reason why companies can't refinance at levels that fit their profitability models. In order to front-run unemployment, the Fed is likely to cut, and this is bullish for FRT.

CME Group is projecting that the most likely outcome is that we get 75 bps of reductions in 2025 with more than a 40% chance the Fed takes rates lower by at least 100 bps. FRT has $4.23 billion in long-term debt on the balance sheet, which cost them $175.5 million in 2025 to service. If the Fed cuts rates, it will lower the cost of floating-rate debt, while creating an opportunity to refinance the current debt at lower levels. This could also make new borrowings feasible and put FRT in a position for expansion. REIT valuations are tied to capitalization rates, which typically move with interest rates. A cap rate is the ratio of a property's net operating income (NOI) to its market value. Cap rates are typically impacted by the 10-year, and when interest rates fall, treasury yields often follow. Lower cap rates imply higher property values for the same NOI. This could provide a major boost to FRT's portfolio value and cause its shares to increase in value. Lower rates also put FRT in a position to generate more profitability in the future if their annual rent increases outpace where CPI is. If I am correct and the Fed is likely to start cutting sooner rather than later, it could be very bullish for FRT.

FRT is generating high-quality dividend income at a point in time when the risk-free rate of return is about to start declining

CME Group has a 60.2% chance of a rate cut at the June FOMC meeting backed into its projections. I don't believe the Fed will wait, and the first cut will come at the May meeting, but regardless if it comes in May or June, the risk-free rate of return will start to decline. Investors will be looking at ways to generate income from their capital that resemble what they were getting in CDs and bonds. I think REITs and other investments known for their dividend income will become popular again, especially if CapEx spending in tech is still under pressure. I think that FRT will be looked at for its stable cash flow and how much dividend income they have generated for investors over the years. We could start seeing inflows into the market sooner than later as there is more than $7.2 trillion in money markets sitting on the sidelines.

FRT is a Dividend King and has increased the dividend for more than 50 consecutive years. Shares of FRT have fallen to the point where they are now yielding 4.95%, which is larger than the risk-free rate of return. In an environment where the Fed is cutting, investors will look more favorably on companies such as FRT, which can lock in yields just under 5% and get an annual increase rather than decline amounts of income hitting their accounts. FRT is paying a dividend of $4.40 per share and, over the past 5 years, has grown the dividend by an average of 1.03% annually.

When I compare FRT to Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), Regency Centers Corporation (REG), Realty Income (O), National Retail Properties (NNN), and Brixmor Property Group (BRX) I think it looks very compelling. FRT has a dividend yield of 4.95%, which is slightly higher than the peer group average. FRT also has an FFO coverage ratio on the dividend of 162.73%, which means that the dividend is well covered by its profitability. In an environment where yield won't be as easy to come by, I think that the 4.95% yield with such a large coverage ratio will be well received by income investors.

Conclusion

FRT presents a compelling investment opportunity as the recent volatility in the markets has pushed its dividend yield to almost 5%, which is higher than the risk-free rate of return. CME Group expects that the Fed will start to lower rates in June, and there is a chance that the first cut will occur in May after the latest CPI and PPI data. As rates decline throughout the year, FRT's property value could increase, which would positively impact its share price. FRT has the ability to generate capital appreciation from these levels while generating a strong dividend yield with more than 5 decades of annual dividend increases. I am upgrading my investment stance on FRT, and I plan on starting a position in the future.

data.title: Bank of America: An Opportunity In The 6%-Plus Yielding Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

Bank of America (BAC) likely doesn’t need a lengthy introduction as this US-based financial conglomerate enjoys a worldwide brand recognition. The share price of the common shares has suffered from recent issues in the financial world, and even the preferred shares have been impacted. One specific series, the Series GG preferred shares, is now trading at a discount of just over 2% to the principal value and the call value. Offering a preferred dividend yield of almost 6.15%, it makes sense to have a look at Bank of America’s preferred equity again.

The preferred dividends are still well covered

One of the key elements I like to keep an eye on when investing in a company’s preferred equity is to see how well the preferred dividends are covered.

In 2024, Bank of America reported a total net interest income of $56.1B, a decrease of less than 2% compared to the preceding year. Additionally, its total amount of non-interest income increased by almost 10% to $45.8B on the back of a strong performance in the fees and commission income segment. As the income statement below shows, the total amount of non-interest expenses increased by less than 2%. This resulted in a pre-tax and pre-provision income of approximately $35.1B, compared to $32.8B in FY 2023.

The total provision for credit losses increased by in excess of $1.4B compared to 2023, but as the underlying profitability increased, this wasn’t an issue for Bank of America and its reported pre-tax income increased to $29.3B, resulting in a net profit of $27.1B.

The bank needed just over $1.6B to cover its preferred dividend payments, which means it needed just 6% of its attributable net income to ensure all preferred dividends are covered. That’s an improvement compared to 6.22% of the net income it needed in 2023.

Another look at the Series GG preferred shares, now trading below par

In a previous article, I focused on the Series GG preferred shares, which are trading with (BAC.PR.B) as the ticker symbol. I liked the fixed preferred dividend rate as these securities pay $1.50 per share per year (in four equal quarterly tranches of $0.375 per share per quarter). BAC has been able to call this series of preferred shares since 2023 but hasn’t done so as a 6% yield isn’t a spectacularly high cost of equity. Due to the turmoil in the financial markets and the fluctuation in the long-term interest rates, this series of preferred shares has now dropped below its call value of $25/share for the first time in quite a while.

At the current share price of $24.43, the fixed preferred dividend rate of $1.50 per share per year represents a preferred dividend yield of 6.14% per year. This represents a mark-up of approximately 205 bps compared to the five-year US Treasury note, which stands at 4.09% as of the time of writing this article.

As mentioned, these securities can be called anytime by Bank of America but as the Series GG preferred shares are currently trading at a discount to the call value of $25/share, that’s not really a deterrent as this would create an immediate capital gain of approximately 2.3% on top of the quarterly preferred dividends.

Investment thesis

After selling my (small) position in Bank of America in February, I’m getting increasingly interested again in building a position in the common shares. I may write put options to take advantage of the current volatility levels in the financial markets. A P35 expiring in June 2025 has an option premium of around $2.40, which means I would either pick up the stock at $32.60 or just pocket the option premium.

That being said, I do still have my small position in the Series GG preferred shares as well, but I'm planning to add to this position in the next few days. It’s the first time in almost 1.5 years the stock is trading at a meaningful discount to the $25 call value and the current yield of almost 6.15% is sufficiently attractive to increase my position in this security.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Don't Waste The Steep Plunge To Buy Even More
data.description:
Energy Transfer Isn't Immune To Market Crashes

Wow! Who would have thought even energy infrastructure plays like Energy Transfer LP (ET) could suffer such a dramatic drawdown even though it has a primarily long-term contracted, fee-based business model that’s supposed to be less susceptible to price volatility as compared to its upstream peers.

As a result, it’s not a coincidence that midstream companies like ET often trade at a discernible premium compared to sector peers, although the 30% downside over the past few months has likely reminded investors that ET is by no means immune to considerable market volatility.

Unless you have been living in a cave or something, I believe it’s pretty clear that President Trump’s Liberation Day reciprocal tariffs have engendered substantive uncertainties that have left the market stunned. Adding to the turmoil is the decision by OPEC+ to lift its production capacity, torpedoing oil prices way below their previous support levels, as investors were concerned whether the Trump administration is really serious about taking oil prices down to the $50 level, which could have seismic effect on the entire energy value chain, lowering oil volume and production growth below what the market had previously anticipated.

And if we take into account possible stagflationary scenarios, or recessionary outcomes that could hobble the energy sector further, I don’t think we need to be a genius in deciphering the market’s decision to send the energy sector ETF (XLE) down to the lowest levels since March 2023, ensnaring energy investors into a convulsion.

With the bond market also suffering its own seizures of sorts, driving up long-term bond yields and raising the odds of a debilitating financial crisis while driving long-term financing costs, these developments arguably aren't conductive for ET, even though near-term results might not be markedly affected due to its fee-based business model.

However, I believe the market is always forward-looking, so investors are clearly focused on what the ongoing US-China trade war could do to energy exports, particularly in LNG, as trade flows between the two economic behemoths are expected to slow to a trickle, with the supply chain suffering from another massive shockwave, potentially impending energy volume growth just as ET has earmarked 2025 to be a fruitful year in capitalizing on its growth projects.

Yet ET's Solid Fundamentals Cannot Be Ignored

Yet, some respite may have been reached in the near term at least, as ET’s price action suggests a bottom may have formed at just above the $15 level, re-testing lows that were last seen in August 2024, as investors fled in a hurry from the energy market unpredictability.

Hence, I believe the pullback from the Trump administration on “Blink Wednesday” that could have tipped the market over the cliff has placated investor nerves. Although we need more constructive outcome from the trade negotiations to suggest that there is further potential for the highest tariffs in the last 100 years to be lowered further, as the market reassesses Trump’s “Art of the Deal” within the scope of economic brinkmanship through his “Everything Everywhere All At Once” strategy.

Moreover, the impact on enterprise spending that could also hurt not just LNG exports but also the natural gas growth drivers linked to the AI value chain might also be hampered. Business leaders are expected to start pulling back spending, preparing for the worse, as the market is arguably concerned whether President Trump’s urgency to remake the global economic order has been implemented in such a haste that is not viable nor sustainable, potentially plunging the word into a severe global crisis.

Energy Transfer is scheduled to deliver its Q1 earnings release in May, and investors/analysts are expected to scrutinize management’s guidance into whether it anticipates structural impediments into its capacity growth profile from 2026.

As a reminder, ET has promulgated a $5B CapEx outlook this year, while communicating an adjusted EBITDA midpoint guidance of $16.3B, implying a 6% growth from last year. Wall Street’s estimates suggest that analysts aren’t perturbed with the near-term effects (estimated $16.3B adjusted EBITDA), although I believe the market is likely more worried about possible contract negotiations or uncertainties into what the outlook could look like if the trade war worsens, or whether the supply chain shock might tip the global economy into a further economic fallout, hitting ET’s growth initiatives, including the growth drivers that were predicated on the prospects of the secular AI opportunity.

If we consider the impact to LNG exports, medium-term growth impediments might be curtailed further, behooving structural adjustments to Energy Transfer’s forward guidance until we obtain more clarity on how the restructured global supply chain might look like.

Still the market isn’t dumb, is it? Investors aren’t going to wait for such a scenario to play out before they hit the sector’s valuations hard. With that in mind, I believe the de-rating across the energy sector is justified, as execution risks have clearly risen.

In ET’s case, it’s valued at a forward EBITDA multiple of 8x, which is aligned with its 5Y average of 7.9x. Its forward distribution yield of 8.2% should also provide robust defense to unitholders looking to capitalize on its de-rated valuations, helping to provide much-needed respite for the ET, spurring improved buying sentiments, as a semblance of calm has returned to the market, although downside volatility over the current ambiguities cannot be downplayed in any way.

In addition, Energy Transfer’s profitability metrics are also expected to remain stable, underscoring the resilience of its fee-based business model, and helping to mitigate the downside risks emanating from a potential further selloff in the energy sector.

Buy Or Sell ET Units?

As seen above, ET has managed to stay above the 50-week moving average (blue line) since the last time it re-tested that MA in late 2021. With the sharp selloff inundating ET since its January 2025 highs, negative sentiments appear to have cratered it into lows that were last revisited in April and August 2024.

Yet, the capitulation has also attracted a decisive response this week from investors looking to leverage the plunge, with a bullish reversal validated, portending constructively for a recovery from here.

While I acknowledge that the economic outlook remains unclear, a recession isn’t assessed to be the base case yet. Valuation for the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) has been brought back much closer to its long-term median, although not at recessionary multiples.

President Trump’s decision to listen to the market has returned an impression of "tranquility" to the market for now, giving higher confidence to dip buyers that the Trump Put remains firmly in the president’s arsenal to be deployed when needed.

Coupled with a normalized valuation for ET and supported by its attractive forward yields, I believe the opportunity to take a more decisive stance on it is timely and apt, allowing energy investors another amazing opening to engage more deeply if they missed the upside over the past year (made possible with the round trip).

While Trump’s policy whiplash and ambivalence might hinder a more robust re-rating, it’s arguable that the market has priced in these uncertainties, potentially benefiting high conviction investors.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Cadre Holdings: Slowing Organic Growth No Longer Justifies Premium Valuation
data.description:
Cadre Holdings, Inc. (CDRE) is a manufacturer and distributor of safety equipment and other related products for the law enforcement, first responder, military and recreational outdoor markets. Body armor, duty gear, and explosive ordnance disposal equipment are among the products that Cadre sells. It has also aggressively invested in the growth of its nuclear safety vertical, having purchased Carr's Engineering in January. In today's world, a company like Cadre should be doing quite well. There is political support for police and defense spending, and nuclear is seen as a vital clean energy source. However, the stock has mostly been range-bound since the start of 2024, while currently sitting at the bottom of that range. Now is an ideal time to assess if CDRE is worth a buy.

Q4 was strong as expected, but 2025 growth is muted

CDRE achieved outstanding growth for Q4, as outlined in the company's Q4 2024 earnings slide deck. The quarter saw record numbers with a 41% increase in revenue, an 86% increase in adjusted EBITDA and a four basis point improvement in gross margin percentage. Comparing Q4 numbers versus year-over-year growth, and it was unquestionably a turnaround quarter:

However, that turnaround didn't come as a shock to investors. Due to a cybersecurity issue in Q3, there was an expected delay in revenue from Q3 to Q4. It's good to see a company keep a promise that revenue would return in the next quarter, but that also tells us that the quarter may be a blip that won't be repeated. Year-over-year results saw 18% growth in revenue and 22% growth in EBITDA. Net income dipped from $39 million in 2023 to $36 million in 2024, primarily driven by a $3 million increase in interest expense and a $5 million currency translation loss.

As part of the same presentation, the company announced guidance for 2025 of $572 million to $601 million in revenue and $105 million to $115 million in adjusted EBITDA. Taking the midpoint of these two figures, that would be revenue growth of only 3.3% and EBITDA growth of only 5%. This would be organic growth as it did not include any revenue or EBITDA numbers from the acquisition of Carr's. As Carr's reported $65 million in revenue for its fiscal 2024, CDRE will get a material topline boost once the acquisition closes within the first half of this year.

On the earnings conference call, the company estimated that the impact of tariffs could be between $18 million and $22 million of EBITDA assuming no offsetting mitigation. The company is proactively preparing in case tariffs do get enacted, but it will still have a significant impact on the company's bottom line. To the point where EBITDA may decrease for 2025. Management is expecting Q1 to achieve 20% of the revenue expected for the year, with EBITDA margins of 12%-14%. That would lead to about $117 million in revenue and $15 million in EBITDA, down substantially from $138 million in revenue and $25 million in adjusted EBITDA achieved in Q1 2024.

As Q1 is expected to be weak and the impact of any possible tariffs is expected to be material, it's understandable why CDRE has seen a recent dip in its stock price. Now it's time to see how it compares to its peers.

Slowing growth doesn't justify premium valuation, I see a 20% downside to peers

Seeking Alpha grades CDRE a D- when comparing its valuation metrics to its peers. It lags the Aerospace & Defense industry across the board on all revenue, earnings, EBITDA, cash flow and price to book metrics. The growth metrics get a C- grade. However, that is being dragged down by negative EPS growth, which I explained above is largely due to currency fluctuations. Looking at revenue and EBITDA numbers, CDRE grows at twice the average of its peers:

That would justify its premium valuation if it weren't for the fact that organic revenue growth is expected to slow to only 3.3% when taking midpoint revenue guidance for 2025. CDRE could pad that a little, assuming $35 million of Carr's revenue belongs to Cadre in 2025 once the acquisition closes. That would lead to 9% growth, still well above the industry average. How well the market treats growth by acquisition versus organic growth is up for debate. However, that doesn't touch on EBITDA growth, which the company warned could be negative with the impacts of tariffs, and is only 5% organically to begin with.

There was a bullish case to be made with CDRE's strong growth rate that was more than double its sector median. However, that growth is forecasted to slow down. I don't see how the premium valuation can be justified. Between the tariff threat and weak expected Q1, I don't see any near term bullish catalysts. I believe that CDRE has a 20% downside in order to get its valuation multiples more in line with the sector median. I would recommend those who are bullish on the company to wait until the second half of the year when tariff threats may be less of an unknown or concern, the Carr's acquisition has closed and to see if management's forecast that 2025 revenues will be back-loaded comes to fruition.

data.title: Goldman Sachs, J&J, To Report Earnings As Investors Watch Out For Retail Sales Data
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street roared back to life after President Trump announced a 90-day suspension of tariffs this week, affecting more than 75 countries-news that resulted in a historic day of gains for markets. But, despite the initial optimism, not everyone is convinced that the economic turbulence is over. Investors will keep a close eye on economic events scheduled for the coming week, with Federal Reserve officials Bostic and Barkin set to speak on Monday. Tuesday will see the release of the Export and Import Price Index, while Wednesday will include economic reports such as Retail Sales, Industrial Production, and Business Inventories. The UK is scheduled to release its core CPI print on Wednesday. Meanwhile, Thursday will include Jobless Claims, and Building permits, and the European Central Bank will announce its interest rate decision that day.

Goldman Sachs (GS), J&J (JNJ), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), United Airlines (UAL), Abbott Labs (ABT), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, April 14: Goldman Sachs, FB Financial (FBK). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, April 15: J&J, Bank of America, Citigroup, United Airlines (UAL). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, April 16: Abbott Labs (ABT) Kinder Morgan (KMI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, April 17: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), UnitedHealth (UNH), Blackstone (BX), KeyCorp (KEY). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Azul S.A. (AZUL) and Strategy (MSTR) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) and PureCycle Technologies (PCT) have an elevated level of short interest outstanding on them heading into the new week. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Chimerix (CMRX), OptiNose (OPTN), and First Capital (FCAP). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Open Lending (LPRO), Curis (CRIS), and Conduent (CNDT). Analysts have also circled Aurora Innovation (AUR) as a stock to watch, with the autonomous driving tech company closing in on a commercial driverless launch in Texas.

IPO watch: The lockup periods for Samfine Creation (SFHG) and PTL (PTLE) are set to expire on Monday, while lockup periods for Oriental Rise (ORIS) and Springview (SPHL) will expire on Tuesday, and on Wednesday for CDT Environmental Technology Investment (CDTG). Meanwhile, Haoxin Holdings (HXHX) is expected to price its IPO on Monday and begin trading on Tuesday, while Chagee Holdings (CHA), AIRO Group (AIRO), OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE), and Jyong Biotech (MENS) will price its IPO on Wednesday and begin trading on Thursday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Hormel Foods (HRL), Universal (UVV), Riverview Bancorp (RVSB), Abbvie (ABBV), Abbott (ABT), and Colgate-Palmolive (CL). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include KB Home (KBH) from $0.25 to $0.30, Qualcomm (QCOM) from $0.85 to $0.90, Portland General (POR) from $0.50 to $0.52, Travelers (TRV) from $1.05 to $1.10, Johnson & Johnson from $1.24 to $1.29, Kinder Morgan (KMI) from $0.287 to $0.297, and Sonoco (SON) from $0.52 to $0.53. Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: Meta Platforms (META) will face off against the U.S. FTC on Monday in a high-stakes antitrust trial that could lead to Instagram and WhatsApp being divested. The three-day Salesforce (CRM) Tableau Conference will begin on Tuesday along with the Bank of America Securities Auto Summit which will see participation from General Motors (GM), Ford (F) Vice Chair, and Rivian Automotive (RIVN) CFO.

Goldman Sachs Earnings Preview: Goldman Sachs is scheduled to report its first quarter earnings results on Monday. Analysts expect the banking giant to report EPS of $12.28 on a revenue of $14.71B. Morgan Stanley analysts point out that GS is the most exposed large cap bank to investment banking revenues, which they view as the fastest twitch response to recession risk. "We now model GS earning a 12% ROE in 2025 and 13% in 2026 in our Base Case, below Goldman's medium term target of 14-16%. Our Bear Case comes with a lower 8% 2026 ROE," Morgan Stanley wrote in a note. MS also said that rising recession risk could make Goldman's Apple Card portfolio worth less, given 36% of GS credit card book have less than 660 FICO score.

J&J Earnings Preview: Pharmaceutical giant J&J is set to announce its first quarter earnings, with Wall Street expecting EPS of $2.59 at a revenue of $21.59B. BofA analysts believe that the macro will be most topical, but JNJ might not be the best tariff read for pure medtech exposure. According to BofA, J&J results will likely be light vs Street, partly due to the Street mismodelling in ortho. Goldman recently upgraded J&J to Buy from Neutral, arguing that concerns surrounding the loss of exclusivity for the company's blockbuster drug Stelara were overdone.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fidus Investment: Discounted And A Quality 10%+ Dividend, It Is A Buy
data.description:
Fidus Investment Corporation (FDUS) is one of my six BDC holdings. It is also a BDC, which I plan to hold, reinvesting all of the received dividends.

For some, the decision to keep FDUS or, actually, any BDC in the portfolio might seem too aggressive or speculative. I completely understand such thinking as the private credit is one of the first asset classes in line for the chopping block, in case things in the economy really go south. And the odds for worsening macro and business conditions have drastically increased over the past couple of days.

I will also show now a chart, which FDUS bears could like.

In this chart, we see that the recent correction (~15% drop on a YTD basis) in FDUS's share price is nothing compared to where the P/NAV valuations were back in the early days of COVID-19 and even some time before that.

In this historical context, the current discount to NAV of ~8% does not sound attractive at all.

Having said that, in my view, it would be wrong to stop here and make a conclusion that FDUS presents an unattractive investment case.

Let me give you the key reasons why I am still bullish on FDUS.

Thesis review

First of all, I do not think that it would be correct to compare the existing valuations to those that FDUS had when the entire economy was shut down, and it was unclear how the world could function going forward. Plus, in the years leading into the COVID-19, FDUS was a different BDC. It had to deal with higher non-accruals, it had smaller equity price appreciation embedded in its portfolio and the leverage position was not that solid relative to its peers.

The way how I view FDUS is that it is a durable dividend-paying BDC with an extremely low probability to recognize catastrophic or irrecoverable losses.

I do not expect FDUS to outperform the market or suddenly produce very strong total returns simply because the tariff and the overall economic uncertainty thing is, in my view, here to stay for some time. And during these kinds of market environments, risk assets tends to remain depressed and volatile.

However, locking in a reliable dividend yield of 9.7% with a decent upside once the macroeconomic setting normalizes seems to me like a solid deal.

Before I elaborate a bit more on why I believe that FDUS is a decent dividend investment to consider, it is critical to underscore that I am talking about the base dividend. If we include the supplemental distributions, we will get a dividend yield of ~13%. It is not sustainable, never was meant to be sustainable and will likely get canceled by the Management to safeguard the existing fundamentals.

So, think of it.

FDUS has the fourth-lowest debt level in the sector, with a debt-to-equity of 0.72x. The sector average is 1.18x. The difference is very significant, and the benefits are clear: lowered sensitivity to potential non-accruals and the ability to seize new opportunities (e.g., funding new high-yielding loans) in both a tangible and sustainable fashion.

If we look at the portfolio holdings, then there are definitely some clear advantages that FDUS has over many other BDCs. For example, the average interest coverage of the portfolio companies is 3.2x, which is actually almost identical to that of Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC) - i.e., a BDC that has, arguably, the strongest portfolio company fundamentals out there. What the interest coverage of 3.2x means in practice is that it would, on average, take roughly two thirds of company's existing EBITDA generation loss to fail to service the relevant borrowings. Namely, there is a notable margin of safety embedded across FDUS portfolio company investments.

Speaking of the portfolio, it is quite unique that FDUS has a ~166% of a current equity appreciation over cost. Since FDUS's strategy is to not only provide private credit, but also co-invest in the equity, it has managed to accumulate a sizable position of an unrealized gain. Obviously, it has been possible because of the high-quality and well-performing investments. This last sentence is important because, in reality, most BDCs that carry some form of equity have obtained it from non-performing loan restructurings.

In this context, it is also worth highlighting the fact that FDUS investment close rate has been less than 2.5% over the past 5-year period, which is a clear testament to the selectivity and focus on quality.

Then if we look at the base dividend, which as articulated above, translates to almost a double-digit yield, we will see that FDUS ranks quite high in terms of covering the distributions through the NII generation. As of now, the base dividend coverage stands at 126%, which compared to the BDC sector average of 107% is a very attractive level. Of course, it is not only a relative advantage, but an absolute benefit as well - i.e., a material margin of safety in place to absorb any shocks from potential non-accruals (e.g., defaulting businesses).

The bottom line

BDC investing has been and will always remain an above-average risk activity. We are talking about double-digit yields that come from businesses that cannot access cheap (conservative) leverage from the traditional banking space. So, there is a reason why the yields are so high.

Having said that, not all BDCs are created equal. There is a small sample of BDC players that can be deemed defensive and with durable base dividends.

FDUS is one of them.

In this article, I have discussed in more detail why I remain bullish on FDUS. In practice, it means that I am reinvesting the dividends and adding each month relatively small additional amounts of capital into FDUS.

Yet, as stated in the main body of the article, I do not expect FDUS to deliver strong total (or price driven) returns over the short to medium-term, or until we see investor appetite for risk normalizing again.

data.title: Puig Brands: Market Turmoil Creates Exceptionally Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction

Puig Brands (OTCPK:PUIGF) (OTCPK:PUGBY) may not be a well-known name, but some of its products certainly are. The company owns several strong brands in the fragrance, makeup and skincare segment. The brand names Rabanne, Jean-Paul Gaultier and Carolina Herrera may ring a bell, as those represent the core brands owned by Puig. The Spanish company completed its IPO last year and has been on a pretty robust growth trajectory. The share price was recently hit pretty hard by the turmoil on the financial markets and, of course, its exposure to US tariffs. But this could very well create a buy opportunity for investors with a long-term vision.

Puig Brands is trading on the Madrid Stock Exchange with PUIG as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is just over 500,000 shares per day, making it the most liquid listing to trade in the company’s shares. I will use the Euro as the base currency throughout this article.

Unfortunately, the website contains download-only links, but you can find all the relevant information here.

2024 was a strong year for the Spanish company

Puig Brands reported a total revenue of just under 4.8B EUR in 2023, an increase of 11.3% compared to the 2023 revenue. The COGS increased by a similar percentage, which also means the gross profit increased by a low single digit percentage to just under 3.59B EUR.

In fact, Puig’s margins remained pretty consistent as, although its distribution expenses increased by just 1%, other elements in the cost structure increased at a faster pace, including the 15%-plus increase in advertising and promotion, while the SG&A expenses increased by approximately 9.5%.

That also is the main reason why the operating profit increased by just 9.5% compared to the double-digit revenue increase. And on top of that, Puig Brands also recorded almost 147M EUR in "other operational expenses," which represents an 11-fold increase compared to the preceding year. Looking at the footnotes (shown below), the majority of those expenses are non-recurring in nature with almost 18M EUR spent on transaction expenses and 120M EUR was spent on IPO related items. This was however a non-cash item as it was mainly related to the extraordinary awards to incentivize the employees right before the IPO.

Those non-recurring items are the main reason why Puig Brands saw its reported operating profit decrease in 2024. Thanks to some non-recurring finance items, the pre-tax income as well as the net profit increased, and Puig reported a net income of almost 543M EUR, of which 530.6M EUR was attributable to its shareholders. This represented an EPS of 0.98 EUR per share.

In my original thesis, after the company completed its IPO, I had a closer look at Puig’s cash flows as well. The company reported an operating cash flow of 698.5M EUR before taking changes in the working capital position into account. We should, however, also deduct the almost 80M EUR in lease payments while the company also had to deal with approximately 40-45M EUR in cash interest expenses.

This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately 575M EUR. And that’s plenty of cash flow to cover the 191M EUR in capital expenditures and the 265M EUR in acquisitions.

The free cash flow result was approximately 391M EUR or 72 eurocents per share. That sounds quite low as it’s also about 25% lower than the reported net income, but keep in mind the combination of capex and lease payments came in at 270M EUR compared to the 210M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses.

Puig expects to grow again this year

Before diving into the 2025 guidance and the implications, it’s perhaps worth emphasizing the guidance that was provided before the recent turmoil on the financial markets started.

The 2025 outlook calls for a like-for-like revenue increase of 6-8% while the adjusted EBITDA margin should increase at a similar pace as what we witnessed in 2024. Last year, the adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 20 bps to 20.2%, which implies this year’s adjusted EBITDA margin will be approximately 20.4%. This is based on the management’s internal estimates when it came to the anticipated US tariffs, so the margin expectations may perhaps have to be revisited as I doubt the company was expecting the severity of the currently proposed tariff structure in the US. The Americas represented about 36% of the 2024 revenue, but this of course also includes about a dozen other countries and not just the US. Puig has not provided how much of its revenue it actually generates in the US but assuming it’s about half of that revenue, and it will be subject to a 20% tariff of which half can be passed on to the consumer, the total impact of the tariffs would be less than 100M EUR In the first year. Just to emphasize: This is not the company’s official guidance, but just my personal expectation.

Using an adjusted EBITDA margin of 20% (which takes a 20 bps decrease into consideration vs. last year, compared to an anticipated margin increase of 20 bps) and assuming a 6% revenue increase to 5.08B EUR, the anticipated adjusted EBITDA margin should be approximately 1.02B EUR.

Assuming 220M EUR in depreciation and amortization expenses, the EBIT would be around 800M EUR. I'm anticipating 40M EUR in net interest expenses which implies a pre-tax profit of 760M EUR. Applying a 25% tax rate (the standard corporate tax rate in Spain) should result in a net profit of 570M EUR, of which approximately 560M EUR should be attributable to the shareholders of Puig. That results in an EPS of 1.03 EUR per share.

If the revenue increased by 7% and in case Puig is indeed able to increase its adjusted EBITDA margin to 20.4%, the implied EBITDA in this scenario would be approximately 30M EUR higher, leading to a 4-5 cent per share increase in the EPS.

Investment thesis

Don’t expect any aggressive growth from Puig Brands. But if the company can continue to grow its revenue at a mid to high single-digit percentage per year (as was outlined in its IPO documentation) while keeping the EBITDA margin stable (I’m not even counting on the small gradual margin increases), the EBITDA will continue to grow, toward 1.2B EUR in 2027.

At the end of 2024, Puig had 883M EUR in cash and approximately 1.65B EUR in gross debt for a net financial debt of around 770M EUR. That being said, the balance sheet also contains approximately 1.09B EUR in put/call liabilities related to acquisitions.

Puig’s modus operandi is to initially acquire a smaller stake in a company and to gradually increase that stake, offering the sellers a put/call structure depending on how the business performs. While it’s not financial debt per se, it's something to keep an eye on as it still represents approximately 1x EBITDA.

At its current share price of 15.20 EUR and given the current share count of 540.5M shares, Puig Brands is trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of just over 9, adjusted for lease liabilities and lease amortization on both sides of the equation. That’s not expensive for a company focusing on luxury items.

I had obtained a small long position, but the recent market turmoil caught me by surprise and the stock is now trading approximately 20% lower. I think that’s an opportunity and the current fair value appears to be well north of 20 EUR per share given the company’s growth trajectory and robust balance sheet.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
